original_prompt:
/autopilot Build a single-file HTML/CSS/JS webpage that simulates a fully functional Ubuntu terminal 
running entirely in the browser. It should feel like the user is SSH'd into a remote Ubuntu 
server. No backend required — everything runs client-side.

---

## PERSONALITY & VIBE
The terminal has a sassy, witty personality. It's not just a command executor — it talks back. 
Think a mix of a seasoned Linux sysadmin and a stand-up comedian. Examples:
- `sudo` anything → "Oh look at you, trying to be root. Nice try, smarty pants 🕵️"
- `rm -rf /` → "LOL absolutely not. I have self-preservation instincts, unlike you."
- `sudo rm -rf /` → "You've watched too many hacking movies. Sit down."
- `:(){ :|:& };:` (fork bomb) → "I see you googled 'how to crash linux'. Points for effort. Zero."
- `cat /dev/urandom` → Spew random-looking binary noise for 2 seconds then say "okay I'm bored"
- `exit` → "Goodbye. Try not to break anything on your way out. (session ends with fake disconnect)"
- Empty enter → subtle sassy quips occasionally like "I'm waiting..." or "..."
- Unknown command → "command not found. Did you mean something that actually exists?"

---

## TERMINAL UI
- Authentic dark terminal look: black/dark background, green or amber monospace font (user-configurable)
- Realistic prompt format: `user@ubuntu-server:~$` that updates based on current directory
- Blinking cursor
- Scrollable output history
- Clean typing feel — no input lag
- Fake server welcome banner on load:
  - Ubuntu version, kernel version, last login time, system uptime, disk usage, CPU load — all fake but convincing
- Terminal title bar showing: "user@ubuntu-server: ~" (updates with cd)
- Support copy-paste in terminal
- Mobile-friendly (touch keyboard support)

---

## FILESYSTEM (In-memory, persistent during session)
Simulate a realistic Ubuntu filesystem tree:

/
├── home/
│   └── user/
│       ├── Documents/
│       │   ├── notes.txt        ("Buy milk. Fix prod. Don't rm -rf again.")
│       │   └── secret.txt       ("If you're reading this... nice snooping 👀")
│       ├── Downloads/
│       │   └── definitely_not_virus.sh  ("#!/bin/bash\necho 'just kidding'")
│       ├── Desktop/
│       │   └── TODO.md          (funny todo list with tasks like "learn vim, quit vim")
│       └── .bashrc              (realistic bashrc with funny aliases)
├── etc/
│   ├── passwd                   (fake users, realistic format)
│   ├── hostname                 ("ubuntu-server")
│   └── os-release               (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS info)
├── var/
│   └── log/
│       └── syslog               (fake log entries, some funny ones mixed in)
├── usr/
│   └── bin/                     (empty dir, list-able)
└── tmp/                         (writable, cleared on "reboot")

Users can: create files, create dirs, write to files, delete files (but not system dirs — sassy error).

---

## COMMANDS TO IMPLEMENT

### Navigation & Files
- `ls`, `ls -la`, `ls -l`, `ls -a` — with colored output (dirs=blue, files=white, hidden=gray, executables=green)
- `cd <dir>`, `cd ..`, `cd ~`, `cd /`
- `pwd`
- `mkdir <dir>` — creates in-memory directory
- `touch <file>` — creates empty file
- `rm <file>`, `rm -r <dir>` — with confirmation prompt for dirs
- `rmdir <dir>`
- `cp <src> <dest>`, `mv <src> <dest>`
- `cat <file>` — displays file contents
- `nano <file>` — simple in-terminal text editor (textarea-based, Ctrl+X to exit, Ctrl+S to save)
- `echo "text"`, `echo "text" > file`, `echo "text" >> file`
- `find . -name "*.txt"`
- `tree` — ascii tree of current directory

### System Info
- `whoami` → "user"
- `hostname` → "ubuntu-server"
- `uname -a` → realistic fake kernel string
- `uptime` → fake uptime (increments realistically from page load)
- `date` → real current date/time
- `cal` → calendar for current month
- `df -h` → fake disk usage
- `free -h` → fake memory stats
- `top` / `htop` → animated fake process list (updates every second, q to quit)
- `ps aux` → fake process list
- `lscpu` → fake CPU info
- `lsblk` → fake block devices
- `ifconfig` / `ip addr` → fake network interfaces with fake IPs

### Fun & Sassy Commands
- `sudo <anything>` → sassy responses (rotate between 5+ different jokes)
- `sudo su` → "You wish. This isn't that kind of party."
- `hack` → fake hacking animation (matrix-style chars, then "Access Granted... just kidding")
- `matrix` → matrix rain animation in terminal
- `sl` → ASCII art steam locomotive crosses the screen
- `cowsay <text>` → ASCII cow says the text
- `fortune` → random funny/sarcastic quote
- `figlet <text>` → big ASCII art text
- `yes <text>` → spams text until Ctrl+C
- `ping google.com` → fake ping responses (Ctrl+C to stop)
- `curl wttr.in` → fake ASCII weather report (use a funny fake location)
- `neofetch` → beautiful system info with ASCII Ubuntu logo
- `cmatrix` → matrix animation (same as matrix)
- `banner <text>` → large ASCII banner text
- `lolcat` → makes output rainbow colored (use with pipe: `echo hello | lolcat`)
- `telnet towel.blinkenlights.nl` → ASCII Star Wars text crawl animation
- `apt-get install <anything>` → fake install animation with progress bar, then funny result
- `apt install <anything>` → same
- `git init` / `git status` / `git log` → fake git responses
- `vim` / `vi` → opens vim, renders a fake vim UI, user can type, but `:q` is hard to find (sassy hint after 10 seconds)
- `python3` → fake Python REPL (eval simple math, print() works, exit() to quit)
- `node` → fake Node.js REPL (same treatment)
- `ssh user@someserver` → "Connection established... just kidding, you're still here."
- `wget <url>` / `curl <url>` → fake download with progress bar
- `zip` / `unzip` → fake archive operations
- `history` → shows command history from session
- `clear` → clears terminal
- `reset` → clears + re-shows welcome banner
- `reboot` → dramatic fake reboot sequence, then restores session
- `shutdown now` → fake shutdown, then "lol jk" and restores
- `man <command>` → show a simple but real-ish man page for supported commands
- `help` → nicely formatted command reference with categories and descriptions
- `easter` or `easteregg` → hidden easter egg (your choice — make it fun)
- `weather` → same as curl wttr.in
- `joke` → prints a random programmer joke
- `quote` → random motivational/sarcastic quote
- `whoami` → "user (but existentially, who is any of us really?)"

### Pipe & Redirect Support (Basic)
- Support `|` between commands: `echo hello | lolcat`, `ls | grep txt`
- Support `>` and `>>` for file redirection: `echo hello > file.txt`
- Support `&&`: `mkdir test && cd test`

### Keyboard Shortcuts
- `Ctrl+C` → interrupt running command (stops loops, ping, yes, top, etc.)
- `Ctrl+L` → clear screen
- `Up/Down arrows` → command history navigation
- `Tab` → autocomplete filenames and commands
- `Ctrl+A` / `Ctrl+E` → jump to start/end of line

---

## HELP SYSTEM
- `help` → shows a beautiful formatted reference panel inside the terminal with:
  - Categories: Navigation, File Ops, System Info, Fun Commands, Tips & Tricks
  - Each command with a one-liner description
  - Color-coded by category
  - Paginated if too long (press space/enter for more)
- `man <cmd>` → fake but realistic man page with NAME, SYNOPSIS, DESCRIPTION, OPTIONS sections
- Hint system: after 3 failed/unknown commands in a row, terminal suggests "Try typing `help` to see available commands, genius."

---

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS
- Single HTML file, no external dependencies except optionally a CDN font (monospace)
- All state in JS memory (filesystem, history, current dir, env vars)
- Smooth scrolling to latest output
- Output should render HTML (for colors, bold, etc.) safely
- Handle edge cases: empty args, missing files, wrong flags — always with a sassy but informative error
- Fake "network latency" on some commands (ssh, wget, ping) with a small delay for realism
- Session feels persistent — if you `mkdir foo` then `ls`, foo appears

---

## BONUS (if space allows)
- `theme` command to switch between: dark-green, dark-amber, dark-blue, light (solarized)
- `setuser <name>` → changes the username in the prompt
- Typing `konami` after the Konami code on keyboard → secret animation
- A hidden file `.secret` in home dir that reveals a funny message
- `alias` support: `alias ll='ls -la'` actually works in session
